title: Linear & Quadratic Equations
blurb: Solve linear, system, and quadratic equations, plus functions — the algebraic engine of GRE Quant.
theory:
What an Equation Is

An equation states that two expressions are equal. To solve means to find the value(s) of the variable that make the statement true. The golden rule: whatever you do to one side, do to the other — add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides by the same nonzero amount.

One-Variable Linear Equations

A linear equation has the variable to the first power only (no x2, no √x). Strategy: (1) clear parentheses, (2) collect variable terms on one side and numbers on the other, (3) divide by the coefficient.

Example: 3(x − 2) = 2x + 5 → 3x − 6 = 2x + 5 → x = 11.

Equations with Fractions

Multiply every term by the least common denominator (LCD) to clear fractions immediately.

Example: x/3 + x/4 = 7. LCD = 12, so 4x + 3x = 84 → 7x = 84 → x = 12.

Translating Words into Equations

Build equations from English using a dictionary of keywords.

WordsMath
is, was, will be=
more than, sum, increased by+
less than, difference, fewer−
of, product, times×
per, ratio, quotient÷

Watch order with "less than": "5 less than x" is x − 5, not 5 − x.

Systems of Two Linear Equations

Two equations, two unknowns. Two methods:

  • Substitution: solve one equation for a variable, then plug into the other. Best when a coefficient is already 1.
  • Elimination: add or subtract the equations to cancel one variable. Best when coefficients line up or can be scaled to match.

Example (elimination): 3x + 2y = 16 and 5x − 2y = 8. Add them: 8x = 24 → x = 3. Then 9 + 2y = 16 → y = 3.5.

No Solution vs. Infinite Solutions

Geometrically, each linear equation is a line. The system's solution is where they meet.

  • One solution: lines cross once (different slopes).
  • No solution: parallel lines — same slope, different intercept. Example: x + 2y = 3 and x + 2y = 8.
  • Infinite solutions: the same line written twice. Example: x + 2y = 3 and 2x + 4y = 6.

Quadratic Equations by Factoring

A quadratic has the form ax2 + bx + c = 0. First get one side equal to 0. Then factor and use the Zero Product Property: if A × B = 0, then A = 0 or B = 0.

Example: x2 − 5x + 6 = 0 → (x − 2)(x − 3) = 0 → x = 2 or x = 3. To factor x2 + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b.

Difference of squares: x2 − 9 = (x − 3)(x + 3). Watch for missing terms: x2 = 5x → x2 − 5x = 0 → x(x − 5) = 0 → x = 0 or 5. Never divide both sides by x — you would lose the x = 0 root.

The Quadratic Formula

When factoring is hard, use x = (−b ± √(b2 − 4ac)) ÷ (2a) for ax2 + bx + c = 0.

Example: x2 − 4x − 1 = 0 → x = (4 ± √(16 + 4)) ÷ 2 = (4 ± √20) ÷ 2 = 2 ± √5.

The discriminant b2 − 4ac tells you the number of real roots: positive = 2 real roots, zero = 1 (repeated) root, negative = no real roots.

Sum & Product of Roots

For ax2 + bx + c = 0 with roots r and s, there are powerful shortcuts: sum of roots = −b/a and product of roots = c/a. These let you answer questions without solving.

Example: for x2 + 7x + 12 = 0, the roots sum to −7 and multiply to 12 — no factoring needed.

Functions & Function Notation

A function f assigns exactly one output to each input. f(x) is read "f of x" — x is the input, f(x) is the output. The notation does not mean f times x.

To evaluate, substitute the input everywhere x appears. If f(x) = 2x2 − 3x + 1, then f(3) = 2(9) − 9 + 1 = 10.

Composing Functions

Composition f(g(x)) means apply g first, then feed its result into f. Work inside-out.

Example: f(x) = x + 2 and g(x) = 3x. Then f(g(2)) = f(6) = 8. Note g(f(2)) = g(4) = 12 — order matters.
whatEtsTests:
• Word-to-equation translation: rates, ages, mixtures, and totals disguised as a paragraph — set up the equation correctly and the algebra is easy.
  • Quadratics with two roots: QC questions where a variable squared hides a negative solution, making the answer "cannot be determined."
  • Sum/product of roots shortcuts: asking for r + s or rs without solving the equation.
  • Systems with special coefficients: finding the value of a constant k that makes a system have no solution or infinitely many.
  • Function notation: evaluating f at expressions like f(a + 1), composing f(g(x)), and reading values from a defined rule.
  • Factoring vs. formula judgment: recognizing difference of squares and clean factorings to save time.
tips: Always move everything to one side so a quadratic equals 0 BEFORE factoring — the Zero Product Property only works against 0.
Use sum of roots = −b/a and product = c/a to skip solving when a question only asks for r + s or rs.
To clear fractions instantly, multiply EVERY term by the LCD; this turns messy equations into clean integer ones.
For systems, pick elimination when coefficients already match or cancel, substitution when a variable has coefficient 1.
Check special systems fast: if one equation is a multiple of the other, infinite solutions; same left side but different right side, no solution.
When plugging into f(x), wrap the input in parentheses to avoid sign and exponent errors, especially for f(−2) or f(a+1).
traps: Dividing both sides by a variable (e.g., x<sup>2</sup> = 5x) and losing the x = 0 root — factor instead.
Forgetting the negative square root: if x<sup>2</sup> = 16, then x = 4 OR x = −4, not just 4.
Misreading "less than": "7 less than n" is n − 7, not 7 − n.
Reading f(x) as multiplication f × x instead of function evaluation.
Sign slips in the quadratic formula — the formula has −b, so a negative b becomes positive.

examples.steps:
The LCD of 2 and 3 is 6. Multiply every term by 6:

  • 6 · (x+1)/2 = 3(x + 1)
  • 6 · (x−1)/3 = 2(x − 1)
  • 6 · 2 = 12

So 3(x + 1) − 2(x − 1) = 12 → 3x + 3 − 2x + 2 = 12 → x + 5 = 12 → x = 7.
examples.shortcut: Watch the subtraction: distribute the minus across both terms of (x − 1) before combining, so −2(x − 1) becomes −2x + 2.

examples.q: The sum of two numbers is 23, and one number is 5 more than twice the other. What is the larger number?
examples.steps:
Let the smaller number be x. The other is 2x + 5.

Their sum: x + (2x + 5) = 23 → 3x + 5 = 23 → 3x = 18 → x = 6.

The other number is 2(6) + 5 = 17. The larger is 17.

examples.q: Solve x2 − 5x + 6 = 0, then find the sum of the squares of the roots.
examples.steps:
Factor: find two numbers multiplying to 6 and adding to −5 → −2 and −3. So (x − 2)(x − 3) = 0 → x = 2 or x = 3.

Sum of squares = 22 + 32 = 4 + 9 = 13.
examples.shortcut: Using identities: r + s = 5, rs = 6, so r2 + s2 = (r + s)2 − 2rs = 25 − 12 = 13 — no need to find the actual roots.

examples.q: If f(x) = x2 − 2x and g(x) = x + 1, what is f(g(3))?
examples.shortcut: Do not expand the full composition formula; just evaluate the inner function to a number first, then the outer.

questions.q: Solve the quadratic equation x2 − 9 = 0 for its positive root. Enter the value.
questions.explain: x2 = 9 → x = 3 or x = −3. The positive root is 3.

questions.q: Solve the system 2x + 3y = 12 and x − y = 1. What is the value of x?
Five less than three times a number equals the number increased by 7. What is the number?
questions.explain: Let the number be n. 3n − 5 = n + 7 → 2n = 12 → n = 6.
x2 = 25 means x = 5 or x = −5. If x = 5 the quantities are equal; if x = −5 then Quantity B is greater. The relationship cannot be determined.

questions.q: The roots of x2 + 7x + 12 = 0 are r and s. What is the value of r + s?
questions.explain: Sum of roots = −b/a = −7/1 = −7. (The roots are −3 and −4, which sum to −7.)

questions.q: Which of the following are solutions to the equation x2 = 5x? Indicate all that apply.
questions.explain: x2 − 5x = 0 → x(x − 5) = 0 → x = 0 or x = 5. Do not divide by x, or you lose the root x = 0.

questions.q: For what value of k does the system x + 2y = 6 and 2x + ky = 12 have infinitely many solutions?
questions.explain: Infinite solutions require the second equation to be a multiple of the first. Multiplying x + 2y = 6 by 2 gives 2x + 4y = 12, which matches 2x + ky = 12 when k = 4.

questions.q: The function f is defined by f(x) = 2x2 − x for all real numbers.
questions.info: The function f is defined by f(x) = 2x<sup>2</sup> − x for all real numbers.
questions.explain: f(3) = 2(3)2 − 3 = 18 − 3 = 15. f(−3) = 2(−3)2 − (−3) = 18 + 3 = 21. Since 21 > 15, Quantity B is greater. The answer is B.

questions.q: If f(x) = x2 − 2x + 1 and f(a) = 0, what is the value of a?
